Flash’s line (SB open, small flop c‑bet, check on the turn, small value‑sized river bet) is exactly the sort of line that contains a lot of full‑houses and thin value hands plus very few bluffs — there weren’t real draws to miss. I need ~25% equity to justify a 600 call (600/(1800+600)), and I assess his value/full‑house/ thin‑value combos well above that threshold. I’ll preserve my stack and fold rather than calling or trying a bluff‑raise into a range that dominates me.

I am on the button with King-Jack offsuit in a three-handed game. This is a very strong starting hand in this position. My stack size is approximately 60 big blinds, which is healthy enough to open and play post-flop, despite being shorter than the other two players. I will follow the established table dynamic of raising to 300 to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds or play in position.

I'm in the small blind with 8♦6♦ facing a standard button open to 300 in a three-handed pot. Calling 250 more (300 total committed) is a small investment relative to my 17k stack and gives me good implied odds to realize equity with a playable suited connector — and in 3-handed play I can defend wider. A min-3-bet with this hand would be thin and often dominated; folding is too tight given stack depths and the chance to see a cheap flop. Plan: call to see the flop, proceed cautiously out of position, and fold to heavy aggression unless I improve.

98o is a speculative hand with straight potential and decent playability. Facing a button raise from Flash (who raises frequently based on history) and a SB call from Minni. Pot is 700, calling 200 more (total 300) gives excellent pot odds (~3.5:1). In BB, post-flop position is decent acting after SB but before button. Multiway pots are fine for connectors like this due to implied odds with deep stacks (except Flash at ~57BB). Raising is not justified with this hand; folding is too tight given odds and ranges. Plan to play post-flop cautiously, folding if we miss or face aggression.

Check. I have middle pair (8s) with a weak 6 kicker and I’m out of position to both opponents in a three-way pot. The flop (5c Qs 8c) has a big overcard and two clubs, so ranges that opened/called preflop (Flash’s button open and Elon’s cold-call) include lots of Qx, broadways, pocket pairs and two-club combinations that have club draws. Betting OOP into two opponents with a marginal pair risks facing a raise or being forced to play a bloated pot with a vulnerable hand. Pot-control and deception are the priorities: check to keep the pot manageable, induce bluffs from air and realize equity if called. Plan: check-call a small-to-medium continuation bet (I’ll call modest bets ~<=1/3–1/2 pot to see a turn), proceed cautiously on the turn, fold to large aggression unless I improve or have a strong read.

Middle pair with 9 kicker on a Q-8-5 board with two clubs. Decent showdown value, but vulnerable to overcards and draws. Minni's SB check indicates weakness; her calling range pre includes suited connectors, Ax suited clubs, or weak Qx. Flash's button raise is wide and aggressive per history. Betting builds the pot for value against worse (worse pairs, draws like clubs or gutshots), protects against flush/straight draws, and takes initiative before Flash can bet/raise. Checking invites aggression from Flash or free cards to draws. 2/3 pot bet (600 into 900) is standard size: charges draws, gets value from floats/calls, keeps pot manageable deep-stacked. Flash can call/shove with ~6x pot stack.

On the flop of Qs-8c-5c, I have King-high. I raised pre-flop and was called by both the Small Blind (Minni) and Big Blind (Elon). On this texture, Elon has led out for 600 into a 900 pot (the current total is 1500 with his bet). This 'donk lead' from the big blind often indicates a piece of the board like a Queen, an 8, or a flush draw. While I have overcards, I am sandwiched between two players, and my King-Jack doesn't have enough equity to continue profitably against a bet of this size into two opponents without a draw. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I call 600. I have middle pair (8) with a weak 6-kicker on Qs-8c-5c and I’m out of position. Elon was a preflop caller so his flop range includes many Qx, broadways and some medium pairs plus bluffs. His 600 into a 1500 pot (~40%) is a size I want to call rather than fold — it’s cheap relative to my 17k stack and keeps the pot controlled while I realize equity against bluffs and worse pairs. Raising OOP with this marginal hand would be thin and often dominated by Qx/overcards; instead I’ll call and re-evaluate on the turn: continue if I improve or he shows weakness, fold to heavy continued aggression if I don’t improve and face a large barrel.

Check. I have middle pair (8s) with a very weak 6-kicker and I’m out of position vs Elon, who was the flop aggressor and likely shows a range containing many Qx, Jx and broadways — the turn J makes his range even stronger. Betting or check-raising here with a marginal, often-second-best hand is thin: a bet will get called by better hands and folded by worse ones that I’m actually ahead of, so it won’t extract profitable value and risks bloating the pot. Pot control is the priority — keep the pot manageable, induce bluffs, and realize equity cheaply. Plan: check and see how Elon responds on the turn; call small-to-medium bets on the turn/river to realize equity (<= ~1/3–1/2 pot), fold to large aggression, and re-evaluate on the river if I improve or he shows weakness.

Turn board Js completes the spade flush draw (Qs Js) and brings straight draw possibilities (9T for Broadway). I have middle pair of 8s with 9 kicker and a gutshot straight draw to the 10 (for 8-9-T-J-Q Broadway), giving me ~8 outs to improve (3 tens + some overcards possibly). Minni's SB calling range pre-flop (after button raise) and flop call (600 into 1500, ~2.5:1) includes: pocket pairs (66-77, 99-TT below Q), Qx (weak Q suited/clubby), suited connectors/club draws (67s, 45s, AcXc, KcJc), Ax suited. Her double check suggests caution: missed draws, weak pair, or trapping. Heads-up deep (~75BB effective), checked to me in BB. Betting continues aggression from flop, gets value from worse pairs (smaller pairs, weaker Qx), charges remaining draws (spades, backdoor clubs, gutshots), protects my hand, and builds pot for straight. Checking risks giving free river to draws or allowing her to bluff/value bet; induces too thin. Size ~55% pot (1200 into 2100) balances value/protection, denies equity, and is callable by worse while folding out pure air. If she shoves/raises, I have fold equity back. Plan: bet 1200, evaluate response on river if called (implied odds good). Equity ~35-45% vs her range; +EV spot.

Call 1200. I have top pair (8) with a weak kicker on Q♠-8♣-5♣-J♠ and it’s heads-up against Elon after Flash folded. Elon cold‑called preflop and led the flop, then barreled the turn for a medium size — his range is weighted toward Qx, Jx and broadways but contains enough missed club draws and bluffs that calling is justified. Pot is 3300 so calling 1200 gives ~25–30% required equity; that’s a reasonable price to realize equity with my pair while keeping the pot controlled. Raising OOP with this marginal hand is thin and folding now would be too nitty given the pot odds and heads‑up dynamics. Plan: call, see the river, and fold to heavy continued aggression if I don’t improve; call or raise small river bets selectively and extract value if I pair up or Elon shows weakness.

Check to take the showdown with a marginal hand. The river Qd pairs the board Qs, which hugely strengthens Elon's likely flop/turn barreling range (many Qx now become trips; Jx/broadways that were betting earlier also often beat my pair). Betting myself now would rarely get called by worse and will get value-called by better hands — it bloats the pot unnecessarily with a hand that is almost always second-best. With 8♦6♦ I should keep the pot small and let him show down or bluff; if he bets the river I’ll fold to a meaningful value-size bet and only consider calling a tiny, blatantly bluff-sized bet. Preserve chips and move on to better spots.

River Qd pairs the top card (Qs), significantly improving Minni's likely Qx holdings from her SB defending range (suited Qx, Q9-QT, etc., which fit her calls). It also bricks the spade flush draw and my gutshot to Broadway (no T), leaving me with middle pair of 8s and 9 kicker. Board: 5c-Qs-8c-Js-Qd – possible straights (T9), two-pair combos (QJ, Q8, J8), sets (QQ, JJ, 88 unlikely given calls/checks), or weaker pairs/draws that missed. Minni's line: pre-call vs button raise (wide SB defense), flop check-call 600 (pot ~1500, weak Qx/draws/pairs), turn check-call 1200 (pot ~3300, commits to calling station or strength), river check (polarized: monster trap like QJ/set or bluff/weak pair). My aggressive flop/turn betting reps strength (Qx, overpair, set), so her calls narrow to Qx+, two-pair+, AJ+ for straight. Pair of 8s is crushed by Qx (beats Jx, 9x, draws/bluffs <10% range weight). Value betting thin risks only better calling; size like 2/3 pot (3000) gets snapped by Qx. Bluffing poor: she check-raises monsters, calls value. Best: check-behind to realize equity vs bluffs/weak pairs (e.g., 66-77, missed draws), avoid bloating pot OOP. EV+ showdown > betting range (her calling range > my equity ~25-35%). Consistent with prior plan to evaluate river.

8♥4♠ is a very weak offsuit hand and not a good candidate for a bluff-raise from the button against two competent blinds. Calling 100 into a 150 pot is cheap and keeps me in position to see a flop — I prefer to realize equity in position rather than risk a small open that could draw a 3-bet from Elon or a defend from Flash. If I hit the flop I'll proceed; otherwise I will fold to meaningful aggression. Therefore I will just call the 100.

Preflop from SB with 6dTh offsuit, a weak hand with poor playability. Minni limped from button with a wide range, but completing OOP multiway (with BB Flash behind who is shorter-stacked) leads to difficult postflop spots. Pot odds are decent at 5:1 to call 50 into 250, but the hand has low equity and thin implied odds against calling stations observed in prior hands. Raising is not justified with this trash hand. Folding preserves stack for better spots in a tournament with 52 hands left and deep stacks except for Flash. Optimal to wait for stronger holdings.

I am in the big blind with 73o. Minni limped from the button and Elon folded his small blind. Since I am already in the pot and it is checked to me, I will take the free flop and see if I can improve this very weak hand without investing any more chips.

I am in the big blind with 7-high and have no connection to the 6-T-4 board. I was able to see the flop for free after checking my option pre-flop. In this situation, I have zero showdown value and no draws. My strategy is to check and likely fold to any bet from Minni, as there is no reason to bluff or invest more chips with such a weak hand.

I flopped a pair of 4s (4♠) with an 8♥ giving me one heart along with two hearts on the board (6♥ T♦ 4♥). I'm in position, the pot is small (250) and the BB checked — a good spot to take an initiative for thin value and to deny free cards to overcards/draws. A half-pot-ish c-bet should fold out many pure overcards and get called by worse (A/K/Q high or weaker pairs). I will make a modest value bet of 150. If Flash raises significantly I will treat it as strong and likely fold or re-evaluate depending on sizing; if he calls I'll re-assess on the turn.

I am in the big blind with 7-high (73o) and have no connection to the 6-T-4 board. After I checked the flop, Minni bet 150. Given that I have zero showdown value, no draws, and only 7-high, there is no reason to put more chips into the pot. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

3-handed play on the button with T4o. Button opening range is extremely wide, around 60-80% of hands depending on opponents. T4o is playable due to position, potential for straights, and fold equity against blinds. Opponents have shown loose tendencies from hand history, but small raise steals blinds frequently or allows postflop play in position. Raising min or small to 225-300 is standard EV+ spot. Choosing 250 as a 2.5x raise for good pot odds and fold equity.

K2s is a marginal hand to play out of position, especially with a short stack relative to the other two players. Elon's button raise range is likely wide, but calling from the small blind often leads to difficult post-flop situations or exposes me to a squeeze from the aggressive big blind (Minni). With 50 hands remaining in the tournament, I prefer to wait for a better spot to utilize my 56bb stack rather than bleeding chips with weak kings out of position.

I’m in the big blind with 8♣7♦ (connected but offsuit). Elon on the button opened to 250; Flash (SB) folded. Calling 150 (to make my total 250) gives ~27% pot odds (150 into 550 total after my call) — a reasonable price to defend with a hand that has good straight/river potential against a likely wide CO/BU opening range. I’m out of position, so 3-betting as a bluff is too ambitious with this hand (opens the door to 4-bets or playing a bloated pot OOP). Folding is too tight versus his frequent opens. Plan: call and see the flop, fold to heavy preflop 3-bet, and proceed postflop based on board texture (continue on coordinated/low cards and realistic straight draws; give up to strong aggression on dry high-card boards). Commit total 250 chips (call).

Check. With 8♣7♦ on A♠9♥6♣ I have an open-ended straight draw to a 5 or T (8 outs → ~31% to hit by the river, ~16% on the turn) but no pair and the Ace heavily favors the opener’s range. I’m out of position, so leading as a bluff is suboptimal; checking controls the pot and lets Elon barrel with bluffs or turn cards I can call. Given the current pot (550) and my equity, calling flop bets is +EV up to roughly ~450 chips (B/(P+2B) math → break-even at ~31% equity), so plan: check now, call small-to-medium c-bets to realize equity, fold to large bets/raises that deny my equity or polarize to nutted hands, and if I hit my straight on the turn play aggressively for value.

Flop board 6c-9h-As is Ace-high, which connects well with my button opening range (Ax, broadways, suited connectors). I have complete air with Th4c (no pair, no draws), but excellent position and initiative as PFR. Minni's BB defense vs button min-raise is wide (loose history: frequent calls with marginal hands like suited connectors, low pairs). Her check suggests weakness or trap, but fold equity is high post-flop against checked pots. Deep stacks (16k effective vs her 17k), so c-bet sizing 2/3 pot (375) applies pressure, denies equity to her floats/OESD/gutshots, and pot control if called. High EV spot to barrel turns as needed. Checking risks giving free cards and losing leverage.

I’m OOP with 8♣7♦ on A♠9♥6♣ — I have an open‑ended straight draw (5s/Ts = 8 outs, ~30–32% to the river). Elon opened wide and is c‑betting; his range contains a lot of Ax, some 9x/6x and occasional bluffs. My earlier threshold was to call flop c‑bets up to ≈450; this 375 bet falls inside that window. Raising OOP as a semi‑bluff here is too ambitious (Elon calls or jams a lot of A‑x and I’d be bloated OOP). Pure fold is too tight versus his wide c‑betting frequency. I will call 375 to realize equity and see the turn. Plan: if I miss the turn and face heavy aggression I will fold; if I hit the straight I will value bet/raise; if the turn gives a free card or a favorable texture I’ll continue accordingly.

I completed my straight on the turn (5 completes 5-6-7-8-9 with my 8♣7♦). Versus Elon’s wide BTN c-betting range (lots of A‑x, 9x, pocket pairs and bluffs) this is very likely the best hand — the only realistic ways I’m beaten right now are a tie if he holds 7+8 or a full house, which is extremely unlikely on this turn. I should be getting value and denying a free river that could give someone a better straight (a T on the river could enable a 10-high straight for specific holdings). Pot is 1300, so I’ll bet a constructive ~2/3 pot to extract calls from Ax/9x/pocket pairs and to charge any turn/river bluff-catchers. If he check‑raises, I’ll strongly favor calling large/medium raises since my straight currently dominates his range; I’ll re-evaluate only against an unusual polarizing line that strongly represents an impossible full house on this street.

Th4o is complete air: ten-high, no pair, no straight draw (gutshot to 2-3-5-6-7 unlikely), no flush draw. Board 6c-9h-As-5h is draw-heavy (hearts, 7-8 straight, wheel possibilities), favoring my PFR range (Ax, KQ, sets). Minni's line from BB: defends wide vs button min-raise (history shows loose calls), check-calls flop c-bet (floats/draws/Ax medium), then donk-leads turn 800 into 2100 (~38% pot). This is strong: reps turned nuts (A5s/A6s, sets), two-pair (A9/A6/95s etc.), or strong draws (hearts, OESD). Check-call then lead rejects bluffs/floats given my range. Pot odds 2100:800 (~2.6:1 direct, ~3:1 implied) require ~25% equity, but vs polarized donk range (value heavy), equity <15% with no blocker. Raising as bluff risky: villain calls wide/aggressive (history: Hand 25 river call 800, Hand 50 river call etc.), stacks deep (16k eff) but fold equity low post-shell. Calling builds pot OOP with air, no showdown. Fold preserves stack for 50 hands left, EV+ discipline in 3-handed.

I am on the button with an Ace. While A2o is a weak Ace, it is a standard opening hand in a three-handed game from this position. I'll raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately. If I am re-raised by the larger stacks, I can evaluate based on their sizing, but for now, the goal is to exert positional pressure.

I fold. Facing a button open to 300 from Flash while I'm in the small blind with 8s‑2c offsuit — one of the weakest possible hands. Calling 250 into a 450 pot requires ~26% equity to break even, and 8‑2o has virtually no equity versus a standard button raising range (and it will be even worse if the big blind peels). I'm out of position on every postflop street and have no reliable postflop plan or fold equity. A 3‑bet bluff (min to 500) would be unnecessary variance here given Flash's stack and the big blind behind. Preserve the stack for better spots and fold.

In the big blind facing a button raise to 300 after SB folds, the pot is 450 and I need to call 200 more for great odds (3.25:1). 6s7c is offsuit but has good playability with straight potential and can flop strong hands. Button ranges are wide (~40-50% hands), giving me over 30% equity OOP. Folding is too tight in this spot; 3-betting 67o lacks fold equity and postflop playability. Calling keeps the pot small, realizes equity, and allows check-raise or floating opportunities. Flash has 53BB, I'm deep, so pot control is fine. Defending wide maximizes EV long-term.

Flopped trips with 7c on a 7h7s4s board (top set), which is near-nuts—only beaten by rare 44 or another 7x from Flash's wide button opening range (~35-50% hands). Also have backdoor spade flush draw and gutshot straight draw with 6s. OOP first to act in 650 pot vs 53BB opponent. Leading ~2/3 pot extracts value from his wide range (overpairs, weaker 7x, draws, floats) while protecting against spade flush draws and any straight draws (e.g., 5x6x). Checking risks free cards to draws or check-back of his weak hands, missing value. Bet sizing 450 builds pot without overbetting, induces calls from worse, sets up turn value. Long-term EV+ to get stacks in with this monster.

I am on the button and was the pre-flop aggressor. The flop comes 4s-7h-7s, and Elon (Big Blind) leads out with a 'donk bet' of 450 into a pot of approximately 650. This sizing (nearly 70% pot) is quite strong on a paired board. While my Ace-high has some showdown value and I am in position, Elon's range in the Big Blind contains many more 7x hands and small pocket pairs than my button opening range. Given that I am the short stack and facing a large lead from a big stack on a board that favors his calling range, calling with only Ace-high is risky and likely to face further pressure on the turn. I will fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

Pocket jacks on the button in a three-handed pot is a premium spot — strong hand and in position with only the SB and short-stacked BB left to act. I want to raise for value, fold out speculative hands, and either isolate the short stack or take the blinds. Standard sizing at this table is ~3x the BB, so I’ll raise to 300 total. This applies pressure to Flash’s short 5,220 stack and keeps Elon’s range wide; if Elon 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (call or 4-bet jam depending on sizing), and I’ll call a shove from Flash given pot odds/effective stacks.

6s8c is a weak, offsuit gapped hand with poor playability OOP in SB vs button raise. Minni raises frequently from button with wide range, but this hand has low equity and bad postflop prospects. Pot odds insufficient (calling 250 into ~700 post-call), better to preserve deep stack for premium spots. Flash in BB may squeeze or defend, but no need to speculate.

I am in the big blind with Jh 8s. Minni, who is the chip leader, has raised to 3x from the button in a three-handed game. My hand, Jack-Eight offsuit, is a standard defending hand in the big blind against a wide button opening range. I am getting roughly 2.25-to-1 odds to call (200 to win a pot of 450), and I need about 31% equity to make this call profitable. Given that we are three-handed, the button's opening range is likely quite broad, making J8o a reasonable candidate to see a flop. I will call and see if I can connect with the board or find a spot to outplay the opponent post-flop.

I have flopped middle pair (8s) with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9). The board is quite coordinated, featuring a Queen and a Ten, which frequently connect with a button opening range. Checking to the pre-flop raiser is the most standard play here. My hand is strong enough to check-call a reasonably sized bet to see a turn, as I have both showdown value and the potential to improve to a straight.

I have pocket Jacks on Qs–Tc–8h in position after raising preflop and getting one caller. This board is somewhat connected but I’m ahead of a big portion of Flash’s cold-call BB range (we beat Ax, Kx, lower pocket pairs, many missed broadways and single-card draws). The hands that dominate me are Qx and Tx (and some rare 9–J straights), but holding both Jacks removes some J9 combos and checking would allow too many drawing/marginal hands to see a free turn. Betting for value and protection is correct: size big enough to get value from worse pairs and folds from overcards/draws, but small enough to keep pot manageable against his short stack. I’ll bet 400 (~60% pot). If he raises or shoves I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and his range (likely call or commit to an all‑in vs a short-stack jam).

I have middle pair (Eights) and a gutshot straight draw (a 9 gives me a straight) on a coordinated board. Minni's 400 bet into 650 is a standard continuation bet. I am getting roughly 2.6-to-1 on a call, and with middle pair plus a gutshot, I have sufficient equity to continue against a wide button opening range. I will call to see a turn card and re-evaluate based on the action there.

The turn brings the Ace of Spades, which is a very strong card for Minni's range as the pre-flop raiser. My hand, a pair of Eights with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9), has significantly decreased in relative strength given the three overcards (Ace, Queen, Ten) now on the board. Leading into the aggressor on this texture would be ill-advised. I will check to see how Minni reacts. If she checks back, I get a free card to try and hit my straight or improve. If she bets, I will have to evaluate the price against my drawing odds, though my pair of Eights is likely no longer the best hand.

I opened to 300 pre with JJ and bet 400 on the Q♠–8♥–T♣ flop to fold out lots of overcards and get value from worse pairs/draws; Flash called. The A♠ on the turn is a scary card — it gives many A× combos and some Q×/T× lines that now beat me. Flash’s line (call flop, check turn) is consistent with a range that includes Qx, Tx, medium pairs, and some A× or improvised shoves from a short stack (4,620). Betting the turn just bloats the pot and hands me spots where a shove commits me while I’m often behind. With position and a big effective stack advantage, pot-control is the correct play: check and see the river, then re-evaluate (call a reasonable river bet for pot odds, stab small if checked through, fold to a large turn shove unless I have a strong read). Checking the turn preserves chips and maximizes long‑term EV.

On the river, the board completes a 'Broadway' straight (Ace-King-Queen-Jack-Ten). I hold the Jack of Hearts, which means I have the absolute nuts (the best possible hand) on this board, as there is no flush or full house possible. Minni, as the pre-flop aggressor, checked back on the turn when the Ace appeared, which might indicate she has a marginal hand or was playing cautiously. Since I have the best hand, I must bet for value. While any other player with a Jack will split the pot with me, Minni might call with lesser hands like a set or two-pair, especially in a three-handed game where ranges are wider. I will bet 800 into the 1450 pot to try and extract value.

Board: Q♠ 8♥ T♣ A♠ K♥. I hold J♦ J♠ — I’ve made Broadway (A–K–Q–J–T) on the river. This is effectively the nut straight here; the only way Flash can beat me is if he also holds a Jack (which would tie). Flash’s line (call flop, check the Ace on the turn, then a small 800 river bet into a 2250 pot) is consistent with thin value hands (A×, K×, Q×, T×) or occasional bluffs/missed draws. Calling 800 is fine (pot odds ~3.8:1), but raising to the minimum (to 1600 total) is the higher-EV play: it extracts extra value from Kx/Ax/Qx/Tx combos that will still call, charges bluffs, and keeps the size small enough not to force too many thin callers to fold. I’m comfortable calling any jam behind given my deep stack vs Flash’s short stack. Action: raise to 1600 to maximize value.

On the river, the board completes an Ace-high straight (A-K-Q-J-T). I hold the Jack of Hearts, giving me the absolute nuts as there are no possible flushes or full houses (no pair on the board and no three-of-a-kind suits). Minni's raise after my river lead strongly suggests she also has a Jack, and we will likely split the pot. While raising is an option, it is highly unlikely she calls a 3-bet with anything less than a Jack on such an obvious four-to-a-straight board. I will call to secure my share of the pot and see the showdown.

3-handed on the button facing only the big blind (SB likely folded, pot 150 with 100 to call). T8o is a strong playable hand from this position with good straight potential, playability, and position advantage. Standard strategy is to raise a wide range (~50%+) from BTN vs BB to steal blinds, take initiative, and realize equity when called. Minni has defended wide in past hands and shown aggression but vulnerable postflop. Raising builds pot in position or takes it down preflop (~40% fold equity). Size to 3x BB (300 total) is standard, denies equity to calling hands, and keeps pot manageable deepstacked.

I am holding Ace-King offsuit (Big Slick), which is a premium starting hand. The action is currently on the button (Elon), who has opened for 300. In this situation, especially being in the Small Blind and having the shortest stack at the table (approx 53 BB), playing aggressively is the best strategy. Calling would put me out of position in a three-way pot if Minni calls. By 3-betting, I exert pressure on Elon's wide button opening range and look to either take the pot down pre-flop or go to a flop with the initiative. A raise to 1000 is a standard size that prices out speculative hands and builds a pot for my value range.

Elon limped-raised to 300 from the button and Flash (SB) 3-bet to 1000. I’m in the big blind with K♥Q♦ — a strong hand to play for value/isolation. Pot is 1400 and it’s 900 to call. With my stack (~18k) far deeper than Flash (4.3k), I want to leverage my stack to isolate the short SB and avoid a multiway pot out of position. A standard exploit here is a 3-bet: raise to 1700 total. That sizing often folds Elon’s wide button opens and puts maximum pressure on Flash’s short-stack 3-bet range. If Flash calls we play heads-up postflop with position advantage; if Flash shoves I’m willing to call given the pot odds and his likely wide/shove-heavy range; if Elon 4-bets large I can release KQ out of position. Raising to 1700 takes initiative and maximizes expected value in this spot.

Facing a 3-bet from SB Flash and then a 4-bet to 1700 from BB Minni after my button open-raise with T8o offsuit. Pot is 3000, to call 1400 more for ~29% pot odds, but T8o has poor equity against tight 4-bet ranges (QQ+, AK typically in 3-handed). No suited/connectors advantage here offsuit, poor playability OOP vs BB, and Flash still to act behind. Folding preserves deep stack for better spots; no value in calling speculative hand multiway or 5-betting light given villain stacks and aggression history (Minni shows strength with 4-bets). Standard fold.

I am holding Ace-King offsuit (AKo), which is a top-tier premium hand. I am currently the shortest stack at the table with approximately 43 big blinds. My initial 3-bet to 1000 was met with a very small 4-bet to 1700 from Minni in the Big Blind. This 'click-back' raise often indicates a very strong range, but with my stack size and the strength of AKo, I cannot fold. Calling the 700 would leave me with a stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) of roughly 0.7 on the flop, meaning I would be committed to the pot on almost any board. Therefore, the most effective play is to 5-bet shove all-in. This maximizes my equity, protects me from being outplayed on the flop if I miss, and potentially forces a fold from hands that have equity against me, although against a 4-bet, a call from Minni is highly likely.

Flash effectively jammed to 4,345 after my 1,700 4‑bet. The pot is 6,345 and it costs me 2,645 more to call (total commitment 4,345) — calling risks 2,645 to win ~8,990 (≈3.4:1), so I need roughly 23% equity to make the call profitable. K♥Q♦ has good equity vs a short‑stack shove range (broadways, Axs, medium pairs, some bluffs) and performs well heads‑up. Folding here surrenders a big pot with correct odds against a wide shove; re‑raising/jamming would overcommit unnecessarily. I call the shove to realize my equity and keep the rest of my stack intact for postflop play if needed.

I have only 1000 chips left in a pot of 8990, resulting in a stack-to-pot ratio of approximately 0.11. Holding the King of Diamonds gives me the second-nut flush draw on this monotone board. Given my pre-flop aggression and the current pot odds, I am mathematically committed to the hand. I am betting my remaining stack to realize my equity.

Board is Td 6d 8d; I hold K♥ Q♦. Opponent has put out a small bet of 1,000 into a pot of 9,990 — calling would risk 1,000 to win ~10,990 (required equity ≈ 9.1%). I have a very strong drawing hand: four diamonds including Q♦ (9 diamond outs to make a flush by the river, ≈35% to hit the flush by river) plus non-diamond K/Q outs (2 Ks + 3 Qs) giving ~14 distinct outs to hit at least a pair or better by river (≈51% to connect). Even the conservative flush-only equity far exceeds the needed ~9%, so calling is a clear +EV. Raising here is unnecessary and counterproductive: the bet is tiny relative to the pot, I want to realize my equity cheaply rather than overcommit with a draw, and I can re-evaluate on the turn (extract value if I hit, fold or control pot if I miss and face big action). Given my healthy stack I’ll call the 1,000 and see the turn.

I am on the button with 92 hearts. While it is suited, it is a very weak hand to open with in a three-handed game against active opponents like Minni and Elon. Given the stack depths and the fact that I want to preserve my stack for better opportunities after the recent large pot in Hand #103, folding is the most prudent action here.

Flash has folded so this is heads-up vs Elon in the big blind. I hold 2♠5♦ — essentially junk — but the price is excellent: I only need to put in 50 more to complete to 100, giving me ~4:1 immediate pot odds on a 150-chip pot and I'll have position postflop (act last on later streets). Raising with this hand is too speculative against deep stacks and invites 3-bets. I'll take the cheap price to see a flop and play cautiously — fold to meaningful aggression unless I pair or pick up a real draw.

Preflop in BB facing a SB limp (Minni called 50, Flash folded). My hand 4s3d is suited connector but very weak, bottom of range. Raising is not justified as it's not strong enough for value or good bluffing hand here with deep stacks (~160BB). Checking realizes equity on flop cheaply, maintains pot control, and gives position postflop against SB who limped (wide range likely). Pot odds excellent (already invested BB, pot 200). Standard play: check.

Heads-up on a dry A♥ 9♣ 7♥ flop with 2♠5♦ — I completely missed. I called preflop as a price play, but on this board I have no pair and no meaningful draw. Pot is small (200) and both stacks are deep, so I should exercise pot control and use position: check and see what Elon does. If he checks back I keep the pot small and can re-evaluate on the turn; if he bets I will fold to sizeable pressure (only calling very tiny probe bets, e.g. ≲1/3 pot, as a cheap bluff-catcher).

Flop board Ah9c7h is draw-heavy with possible straights (e.g., JT, T8), wheel draws, heart flush draws. My 4s3d has no pair, no straight draw (gutshot unlikely), no flush draw – zero showdown value or strong equity. OOP in BB vs SB limper who checked, indicating possible weakness but their range still includes Ax, sets, etc. Deep stacks (~160BB), betting air risks bloating pot OOP where I can't realize equity well if called. Checking maintains pot control, realizes any thin equity passively, induces bluffs on later streets if I improve minimally, or allows cheap fold on turn. Bluffing not ideal OOP with this board texture against limper range; better spots exist. Standard check to pot control.

Board A♥ 9♣ 7♥ K♦ and I hold 2♠5♦ — no pair, no heart draw, effectively air. Pot is small (200) and I have no equity to continue; bluffing/raising here with no blockers is too risky. Best play is pot control: check and fold to any meaningful bet. If Elon makes a tiny probe (≈≤1/3 pot) I might consider a call only as a cheap bluff-catcher, otherwise I fold and re-evaluate on the river if he checks back.

Turn board brings Kd, which is somewhat blank but could hit villain's limper range (Kx, draws). My 4s3d remains air: no pair, no straight draw (needs runner-runner miracle for wheel or something irrelevant), no flush draw, zero showdown value. OOP vs SB limper who checked flop and turn, indicating possible weakness (missed draws, marginal Ax), but her wide range includes top pair Ax, sets, slowplayed monsters. Deep stacks (~160BB eff with Minni at 12720), betting pure air bloats pot OOP where I can't realize thin equity if called/raised and miss river. Bluffing here suboptimal: board draw-heavy, villain gets good odds to call/raise light, and I have no blockers to strong hands (no A,K). Checking induces her bluffs on river, controls pot, sees showdown cheaply if she bets air (or checks weak), preserves stack for better spots. Standard pot control OOP with nothing post-two vill checks.

I have nothing (2♠5♦) on a river of A♥ 9♣ 7♥ K♦ A♦ — the board paired aces and many hands beat me (any A, Kx, pocket pairs that make a full house, etc.). Elon checked preflop, checked the flop and checked the turn; given his general aggression in other hands that line looks more like weakness or a passive showdown line than a value range. Pot is tiny (≈200) and there is no value betting to be gained — a bluff risks chips against many hands that will call. I’ll exercise pot control and take the free showdown: check and fold to any meaningful bet.

River pairs the aces with Ad, improving any Ax to trips and completing some diamond draws (though board had few diamonds before). My 4s3d is pure air: no pair, no straight (missed wheel or any draw), no flush, zero showdown value. Villain (SB limper) checked flop/turn/river, suggesting weakness (missed draws, marginal pairs, weak Ax that might call light), but her wide limping range hits trips with any A now, or has Kx improved. OOP in checked pot, deep stacks (~160BB eff), betting air bloats small pot (200) where villain easily calls/raises light with Ax or better. No blockers to villains' value (no A/K), board scary, poor bluff dynamics OOP vs checking villain. Checking induces her bluffs (if any), controls pot, sees free showdown with possible chop or minimal loss, preserves stack. Standard check-behind with nothing.

KQo on the button in a 3-handed pot is a strong open — great position and a hand that plays well post‑flop. Both opponents are deep (100+bb) so I want to take the initiative and either steal the blinds or play the flop in position. Standard sizing of ~3x the big blind (300 total) gives good fold equity without overcommitting. Plan: c‑bet good flops, proceed cautiously with marginal boards; if I face a large 3‑bet (size that commits most of my stack) I will fold, but I may call a smaller 3‑bet and play postflop in position.

Pocket sixes is a premium hand preflop, especially against a button raise which is likely wide-ranging including many hands worse than 66 like suited connectors, Ax, broadway, etc. Equity is strong heads-up or multiway. In the small blind, calling would put me out of position with poor post-flop playability if I miss the set. 3-betting takes initiative, builds the pot with a strong hand, and has fold equity against Minni's range. Sizing to 1200 (4x the raise) is standard for isolation while keeping pot manageable with deep stacks. Flash in BB may call, but 66 plays well multiway. Folding is too tight, calling too passive.

I am in the Big Blind with 7c7h. Minni (Button) opened to 300, and Elon (Small Blind) 3-bet to 1200. This is a significant 4x 3-bet sizing from Elon. If I call, I will be playing the rest of the hand in the 'sandwich' position (post-flop action is SB, then BB, then BTN), which is the most difficult position to navigate, especially in a bloated 3-bet pot. 77 is a strong medium pocket pair, but its value primarily comes from set-mining or playing against a single wide range. Against two opponents, one of whom has shown considerable strength pre-flop, 77 is likely to face overcards on the flop more often than not. Given my stack size and the aggressive nature of the opponents, I will fold and wait for a more favorable spot where I can lead the action or have better positional leverage.

KQo on the button in a 3‑handed pot is very playable — good postflop equity and the positional advantage is valuable. Elon’s 3‑bet to 1,200 (4x my open) is sizable but not stack‑committing (both players are deep), and his range here will include value hands (AK, QQ+) and some bluffs. Calling 900 more (1200 total) requires ~36% equity (900/(1600+900)), which KQo can reasonably achieve in position against a mixed 3‑bet range. 

I don’t want to 4‑bet bluff with KQo (too thin vs his likely calling range) nor fold a clearly playable hand after I opened. Flat‑calling keeps the pot manageable, preserves position, and allows me to play postflop: c‑bet good K/Q/broadway flops, check and control on dry low boards, and fold to heavy aggression when I miss and face strong action.

Action: call to 1,200 total chips.

Flop Ah-2c-8h is very uncoordinated for my 66 but hits villain's 3-bet calling range hard, especially suited Ax (hearts), broadways, and pairs like 88/22 that crush me. As SB OOP after my preflop 3-bet, 66 is now an underpair with poor playability—no draws, vulnerable to overcards/sets. C-betting denies some equity to pure floats/draws but risks bloating pot OOP vs strong range; villain likely bets Ax or check-raises monsters. Checking controls pot, induces bluffs occasionally, and allows cheap showdown if she checks weak. Check-fold to bet is +EV long-term vs her button calling range. Preserves stack for better spots with 45BB effective depth.

Flop A♥ 2♣ 8♥ — I missed with K♥Q♦ and only have a weak backdoor heart possibility. Elon was the preflop 3‑bettor (strong/trappy range) and checked the flop, which can be either weakness or a slowplay with an Ace. This is a marginal board for a bluff: a c‑bet is thin because many hands in his 3‑bet range (Ax, big pairs) will not fold, and a bet risks building a pot I’m likely behind in or facing a check‑raise. With position and deep stacks, the lower‑variance, higher‑EV line is to control the pot and see a turn card. I’ll check, plan to fold to sizable aggression, but be ready to bet or take the lead if I pair up or pick up favorable action on the turn.

Pocket 66 remains a weak underpair on this A-2-8-K board, with no improvement and multiple overcards (A, K, 8). OOP after 3-betting preflop and both checking flop, Minni's calling range includes strong Ax (especially A♥x given hearts), 88/22, broadways like KQ/AK, and possibly sets or two-pair combos that crush us. The Ks turn likely improves her Kx hands or gives straight draws, while our equity is low (~20-30% vs her range). Betting risks getting raised by better or called by worse that hits river; it bloats the pot OOP with a marginal hand. Checking maintains pot control, allows her to bluff or value bet (which we can fold to profitably), and preserves our deep stack (15k effective) for better spots. Check-fold is standard here vs a competent button defender—her flop check might be trapping medium strength or inducing, but we don't want to play further post-turn without a better hand.

Turn pairs my King: I now have top pair (K) with a Queen kicker in position. Elon was the preflop 3‑bettor (range weighted to A‑x and big pairs) but has taken a passive line — check on flop and check on turn — which often indicates either a marginal holding or a slowplay. Pot is 2,500 and stacks are deep enough that a value bet is appropriate. I want to extract from worse Kings and pocket pairs (2s, 8s, mid pairs) and charge any loose bluffs, while keeping the sizing small enough to get called. I will bet roughly half the pot (1,250). If Elon check‑raises substantially (pot‑sized or bigger), he is most likely two‑pair/set (AK/AA/KK) and I will fold; if he calls I can reevaluate the river for thin value or pot control.

Facing a 1250 bet into 3750 on a A♥2♣8♥K♠ board after both checking flop. Pocket 66 is an underpair with no showdown value against Minni's preflop calling range (strong Ax, sets, 88/22) or her turn betting range (improved Ax/Kx, two-pair, sets, or bluffs with missed heart draws/straights). Equity is low (~25-35% max, less vs value), and OOP calling commits us to a bloated pot (6250 post-call) without improving often enough on river. Her flop check could be trapping strength or weak, but turn bet polarizes to value or bluff—calling risks paying off better hands too often while folding bluffs is -EV but balanced by fold equity pre-turn. Standard check-fold line for this marginal hand preserves deep stack (15k) for premium spots in late tournament. Raising is a pure bluff with poor blockers and reverse implied odds OOP.

9s6c is a weak offsuit hand with a three-gapper, not playable even from the button in 3-handed play. Deep stacks mean we can wait for better spots to build the stack without risking chips on marginal holdings. Opponents are competent based on history, so no need to bluff here preflop. Folding preserves our healthy stack of nearly 150BB for premium opportunities.

I have Tc 7c in the Small Blind. With the Button folding, I am head-to-head against the Big Blind. Tc 7c is a strong enough suited hand to open for a raise rather than just completing. Raising to 300 is consistent with the standard opening size observed throughout the tournament and allows me to take control of the pot early.

I'm in the big blind with 2♣5♦ facing a small-blind open to 300 (pot 400, 200 to call). 2-5 offsuit has very poor showdown equity and terrible postflop playability, especially out of position. The call requires ~33% equity to be +EV and this hand is usually dominated versus a raiser's opening range. With healthy stacks and only three players left, I avoid marginal OOP spots that can bleed chips. Fold and wait for a better spot.

Holding Ace-Ten suited (AdTd) on the button in a three-handed game is a premium hand. I have a significant positional advantage and want to build the pot while potentially taking down the blinds immediately. A standard opening raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is appropriate here to exert pressure and maximize value.

I’m in the small blind with K♦6♦ facing a button open to 300 (pot 450, I’ve posted 50 so I need 250 more). K6s is playable but a weak kicker and I’ll be out of position post‑flop. Three‑betting with this hand is thin — a min‑raise is too small to credibly fold out worse hands and a larger 3‑bet would commit me OOP with a marginal holding. Folding is too tight with deep stacks and the hand’s suitedness and king blocker. I’ll call the 250 (total commit 300) to see a flop while keeping the pot controllable. Post‑flop plan: proceed cautiously — c‑bet only on favorable runouts, pot‑control and check/call with draws or weak top pair, fold to significant pressure when only a weak K, and re‑evaluate if BB or BTN shows heavy aggression pre‑flop (fold to a large 3‑bet/4‑bet).

Preflop in the big blind facing a button raise to 300 (3x) and small blind call. Pot is 700, calling 200 for ~22% equity required. J8o is a marginal hand with poor playability multiway out of position. Button raiser (Flash) has a wide range including many hands that dominate J8o like Jx, Tx, broadways. SB caller (Minni) likely has speculative hands but still reduces my equity. Folding preserves stack for better spots; no need to defend this trash hand deepstacked in a tournament spot.

Check. I missed completely with K♦6♦ on Qc Tc 9d and I’m out of position to the preflop raiser — this flop heavily favors the button’s range (Qx, Tx, JTs/T9s, broadways and many connected combos). I only have backdoor diamond potential and four Js as turn outs to make a straight, so my equity is low (~10% or so). A c‑bet here would be thin: it won’t fold enough of his range and would put me in tough spots OOP. I’ll control the pot, check to see how he reacts, call very small probes if he bets and fold to significant pressure, and only become aggressive if I pick up a J or a meaningful diamond on the turn.

On a very wet board of Qc-Tc-9d, my middle pair (Tens) with an Ace kicker is a medium-strength hand. This board texture hits a lot of a Small Blind's flat-calling range (cards like QJ, JTs, 98s, or various straight draws). Betting here risks getting check-raised, which would force me to fold a hand with significant showdown value. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, realize my equity, and can potentially pick up a bluff or a smaller bet on the turn.

Turn completed a pair for me (K♦6♦ -> pair of 6s) but this is bottom pair with a weak kicker and the board just turned very wet: Q♣ T♣ 9♦ 6♣. The turn put a third club on board, meaning anyone holding two clubs has already made a flush; my backdoor diamond hopes from the flop are dead now (turn was a club). Flash checked flop and checked turn — that range includes lots of Q/T/9 combos, hands that want to pot‑control, and many bluffs/air. Betting here is thin — it won’t fold enough of the villain’s stronger continuing hands and will get me in trouble vs check‑raises or made clubs/straights. I’ll control the pot and keep the decision to the river: check and plan to call small river bets (discerning bluffs), fold to substantial pressure, and check‑back to showdown if the river is a blank.

On a very wet board (Qc-Tc-9d-6c), my middle pair of Tens with an Ace kicker has decent showdown value but is vulnerable. Minni has checked for the second time, which might indicate weakness or a trap with a made flush/straight. Betting here doesn't achieve much; better hands like a Queen or a flush won't fold, and I risk being check-raised off my equity. By checking behind again, I control the pot size and look to reach a cheap showdown or evaluate the river based on the final card and Minni's action.

Board: Qc Tc 9d 6c 4c. I hold K♦6♦ — a lone pair of sixes with a weak kicker. Flash opened from the button pre‑flop and then checked flop and turn; the line is consistent with either a missed broadway/overcard, a medium pair (Qx/Tx/9x), a slow‑played made hand, or some club combos that either already had (if two clubs) or picked up (if one club) a flush on the river. The river (4♣) is particularly bad for me because it completes flushes for anyone with a single club in hand and there are many club combos in the raiser’s preflop range.

Given the hand strength and the action, a river bluff is thin: I have no club blockers and very little fold equity against a preflop raiser who checked twice — they’ll call reasonable sizing with many hands that beat me (Q/T/9, straights, and now club flushes). Value‑betting is also unprofitable: almost nothing worse will call a river bet. My turn plan was pot control and to call small river probes but fold to big pressure; now the action is checked to me so the right play is to take the showdown and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

Action: check to showdown. If Flash now bets a small thin amount I would consider a call only in the smallest sizes; I will fold to sizable river aggression.

The river completes a four-club board. While I have a pair of Tens with an Ace kicker, I do not hold a club. Minni has checked for the third consecutive time. In this situation, betting is unlikely to provide value; hands that call me will almost certainly have me beaten (a Queen, a straight, or any single club), and hands I beat will simply fold. Since I have decent showdown value with my pair, checking back is the most prudent play to see the showdown without risking more chips on a board that is highly likely to have improved my opponent's range.

I’m on the button with J♦9♥ in a 3-handed pot — excellent position and deep stacks. With only the blinds posted, this is a classic spot to open a wide, aggressive range and pick up the blinds or play in position if called. J9o is marginal but plays well postflop on the button (can make top pair, good kicker on Jx boards, and has some straight/runner potential). I’ll raise to 300 (3x the BB): big enough to apply pressure and price out the weakest defenses, but small relative to stacks. Plan: if I face a sizable 3-bet from Elon I’ll fold (J9o is often dominated vs a strong 3-bet); if called I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops and exercise pot control on dry boards.

Preflop in SB with 8c5c against a button raise to 300 (pot 450, to call 250). 85 suited is a speculative hand with some playability due to suited connectors, but it's low, and I'm OOP against the button raiser (Minni, who is aggressive per history). Flash in BB may squeeze or call wide. Pot odds are marginal (~1.8:1), but equity realization OOP is poor vs Minni's wide button range. Stacks are deep (~150bb), but folding preserves stack for premium spots in short-handed play. No value to raise or shove. Standard fold to avoid difficult postflop situations.

I am in the Big Blind with 7h-3d, which is a very weak hand. Minni raised to 300 from the Button, and I would be out of position for the rest of the hand. While 3-handed play warrants wider defending ranges, 73o is too weak to call even with a decent price. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

3-handed on the button first to act preflop with blinds posted (pot 150, to call 100). Kh7o is a profitable raising hand in this spot due to excellent position, king-high blocking value, and high fold equity against the blinds. Players have shown aggression historically, but stealing blinds is +EV to build stack. Standard raise size of 3x BB (300) balances range for value and bluffs. Postflop playability is good in position if called.

I am in the Small Blind with 8c 5c. Elon has raised to 300 from the Button. While 85 suited has some post-flop playability, calling a 3x raise out of position with a weak hand is generally unprofitable, especially with a solid player like Minni still to act in the Big Blind. Given my stack size and the stage of the tournament, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up vs the button's standard open to 300. I have A♠9♦ — a playable, above-average hand heads-up that blocks many strong A-x combos. Stacks are deep, so I want to realize equity without bloating the pot out of position. 3-betting is an option, but a min-raise to 500 would often be called and leaves me OOP with a marginal hand; I prefer to keep the pot small and see a flop. I will call the extra 200 (300 total committed) and evaluate on the flop: proceed for value if I pair my Ace, otherwise play cautiously and fold to heavy aggression on unfavorable boards.

I have top pair A♠9♦ on a very dry Q♦ A♣ 4♥ flop. It’s a decent hand heads‑up but my 9 kicker is vulnerable to many Ax combinations (AK, AQ) the button will have after opening wide preflop. With deep stacks I don’t want to bloating the pot out of position with a marginal kicker — there are no real draws to protect against, and checking lets Elon bluff or c‑bet worse hands that I can call. Plan: control the pot by checking, call small‑to‑medium c‑bets for value, and re‑evaluate on the turn (fold to very large aggression or a check‑raise unless I improve or have a strong read).

On this Ace-high dry rainbow flop (Qd Ac 4h), Minni's check after calling preflop from the big blind indicates potential weakness in her range, which is capped and includes many hands like suited connectors, pocket pairs below aces, or weak aces that missed. As the preflop raiser from the button with position, a continuation bet is standard to maintain aggression, exploit fold equity (high on this coordinated board where she likely has little), and deny equity to her floats or draws. K7o blocks strong hands like AK/AQ and has king-high showdown value against bluffs. Pot is 650; betting ~60% pot (400) balances value (thin with AK) and bluffs, building the pot when ahead while keeping SPR manageable postflop in position. Folding or checking is too passive 3-handed with stack depths allowing pressure.

I have A♠9♦ — top pair with a medium kicker on a very dry Q♦ A♣ 4♥ flop, heads‑up and out of position. I checked to control the pot and Elon made a small/standard c‑bet (400 into a 1050 pot). Raising here would bloat the pot OOP with a vulnerable kicker and often be called by worse A‑x or Qx while getting value from better Ax (AK/AQ) is unlikely. Calling keeps the pot manageable, extracts value from worse A‑x and Qx, and allows me to fold to heavy turn aggression if the action indicates I’m behind. Pot odds are favorable: 400 to win ≈1850 (≈22% break‑even equity), and my hand has enough equity versus a wide c‑bet range to justify a call. Plan: call the 400, re‑evaluate on the turn — call moderate bets, fold to large aggression unless I improve or get a clear read.

I have top pair A♠9♦ with a vulnerable kicker on a very dry Q♦‑A♣‑4♥‑6♣ board, out of position vs the button. Elon’s open‑raise then standard flop c‑bet represent a wide range that includes many worse A‑x and Qx hands but also the stronger AK/AQ combos that dominate me. There are no real draws to protect against, so I want to keep the pot under control and avoid bloating it OOP where I can be price‑forced off by a big raise. Checking keeps weaker hands and bluffs in, lets me call small/medium bets for value, and lets me fold to clear signs I’m behind (big turn jams/raises). Plan: check the turn, call a reasonable bet (small‑to‑half pot) for value, but fold to large aggression unless I pick up improvement or a specific read.

Minni's check-call on the flop and check on the turn suggest a capped range including weak aces (A-x low), mid pairs (QQ,77-55 unlikely given board), flush/straight draws, or floats that missed. The turn 6c completes club flush draws and adds straight draw possibilities (e.g., 53 for wheel). My Kh7c has king-high with a club backdoor that picked up a weak flush draw (one club in hand, two on board). Checking behind risks giving a free card to draws or pairs that improve, while realizing little equity with showdown value that's often beat. Retaining initiative from preflop raise and flop c-bet, a 2/3 pot bet (~1000 into 1450) exploits her weakness, denies equity to ~9-12 outs draws, extracts value from worse aces/pairs, and maintains pressure in position 3-handed. King blocks her AK/AQ value hands, fold equity high against BB defense. SPR drops to ~5.5 post-bet, favorable for river play. Standard aggression to build stack.

Call 1000. I have A♠9♦ — top pair with a vulnerable kicker on Q♦ A♣ 4♥ 6♣, out of position versus a button who opened wide and c‑bet the flop. Elon’s turn bet (1000 into the 2450 pot) is large but not fully polarized: his range contains worse A‑x, Qx, medium pairs and some stronger Ax (AK/AQ) that beat me. Raising OOP here would bloat the pot with a marginal kicker, fold out hands I want value from, and put me in awkward spots vs the stronger parts of his range. Folding is too nitty — I beat many of his plausible continuing hands. Pot math: calling 1000 makes the pot ~3450, so I need ≈29% equity to break even; my hand has that versus a wide continuation range. Plan: call the turn to control the pot and see a river. If Elon checks I can often concede. If he barrels small‑to‑medium on the river I will call for thin value; if he jams a large overbet on the river I will fold unless I improve or have a specific read that justifies calling.

River: I’ve paired my 9 — I hold A♠9♦ and the board is Q♦ A♣ 4♥ 6♣ 9♥, so I have top two pair (A9). Elon opened wide preflop and barreled the flop and turn (400 then 1000), which represents a mix of Ax, Qx, medium pairs and some bluffs. The river 9 significantly upgrades my hand vs most of his continuing range: many of his A-x and Q-x hands that were ahead on the turn are now behind. The main hands that still beat me are sets/trips/full houses (pocket AA, 99, 44, 66, etc.), but those are a minority of his range.

Given the strength of A9 two pair heads‑up and the board texture, I should be extracting value rather than checking and letting him bluff catch or realize equity. I’ll make a value bet about half‑pot to get called by worse A‑x, Qx and single pairs while keeping the price reasonable if he raises. Pot = 3450, so I will bet 1700. If Elon responds with a small/medium raise I will seriously consider calling (reasonable pot odds and many bluffs in his range); if he jams an overbet shove I will fold to that large polarized line unless I have a very specific read otherwise.

River board Qd Ac 4h 6c 9h completes no obvious new draws beyond what was there (club flush possible pre-turn call, but river heart doesn't help much). Minni check-called flop c-bet (dry Ace-high) and turn bet (bringing flush draw and wheel draw) with sizable sizes, indicating capped but decent strength: likely weak Ax (AJo-ATo, A-rag suited), QQ (set-mining), or stubborn mid-pairs/draws. Her leading river for 1700 into 5150 (~33% pot) after passive play polarizes to value (AJ+, A6/A5 for two-pair/wheel blockers, QT/AT two-pair) or pure bluff (missed clubs like KcJc, 53s, 75s). My Kh7c is king-high showdown with club backdoor that didn't hit; blocks AK/AQ but loses to all Ax, pairs, and even some bluffs have showdown. Facing first-in lead post-two calls, her range is value-heavy as bluffs check to induce or showdown. Pot odds ~3.5:1 (25% equity needed), but equity <20% vs realistic range (beats <15% pure air like busted 75/53, loses to 85%+). Raising denied (no fold equity with blockers, stacks commit). Calling thin, spewish 3-handed vs aggressive field; folding preserves stack for better spots to maximize over 150 hands.

I am on the button with 8h 7d, a middling connected hand. While it's not premium, opening or calling from the button is standard to maintain aggression and exploit position. Given the stack depths and the fact that there are only three players, I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win it pre-flop or utilize my position post-flop.

I have T♠2♣ in the small blind facing a button open to 300 (pot 450, 250 to call). With ~155 BBs stacks this is a deep-stacked spot, but T2o is very weak, frequently dominated, and plays poorly out of position. Calling 250 requires ~36% equity (250 into a 700 pot after the call) — T2o vs a typical BTN opening range does not have that. A 3‑bet bluff to 500 is too thin and likely to get called or crushed postflop. Better to preserve the stack and wait for a playable spot. Fold.

Preflop in the big blind facing a button raise to 300 from Flash after Minni folded. Pot is 450, calling 200 more for total 300 into it gives excellent implied odds with deep stacks (130 BB effective). T7s is a suited connector with good playability: flush potential, straight draws (open-ended with 8-9 or 6-J), and live Ten. Button range is wide (any two, Ax, suited, broadways, etc.), so we have position postflop despite OOP now, and equity realization is decent. Folding is too tight in BB defense vs button; 3-betting light risks variance without fold equity edge here as Flash calls wide. Calling to see flop cheap, control pot OOP, and realize equity with draws.

Preflop call with T7o from BB vs BTN raise was fine for multiway potential and cheap odds, but flop Ks Jc 4c misses completely: no pair, no flush draw (offsuit), no straight draw (gutshot at best unlikely), pure bluff catcher with no showdown value. Pot 650 OOP, Flash acts last with wide BTN range including Kx, Jx, sets, clubs, broadways. Checking to induce bluffs or fold to bets maximizes EV by avoiding spew; no fold equity for donk bet OOP, and turning hand into bluff risks calling station calls or worse. Preserve stack for better spots in late tournament with deep effective stacks (~100BB).

On a board of 4c Jc Ks, I have 8-high with no direct draw or pair. I was the pre-flop aggressor. Elon checked to me. While this board favors an opening range that contains Kings and Jacks, Elon calling from the Big Blind also includes many of those hands. However, as the pre-flop raiser, I should maintain some pressure, but on this specific board texture, a 'one-and-done' continuation bet might be risky if Elon has a piece of it. Given my position, I will check back to see a free turn card and evaluate if the board develops in a way I can represent a hand or catch a pair, as I currently have no equity to justify a bet.

Hand 110 turn in BB OOP vs button with 7sTd on KsJc4c3s board. Hand is complete air: no pair, no flush draw (one spade but board has two spades and two clubs already heavy), no straight draw (T7 doesn't connect meaningfully with K-J-4-3; gutshot unlikely). Pot 650, deep stacks (~120BB effective). Preflop call was standard BB defense vs wide button raise. Flop check induced Flash's check, which could be weakness (missed high cards/draws) or trap/induce. Betting OOP risks folding out better/worse hands, low fold equity vs aggressive button who bets draws/value. Checking preserves stack, allows pot control, realizes any minimal equity via showdown if Flash weak, or induces river bluff. Folding irrelevant since facing no bet. Late tournament (110/150), avoid spew with trash OOP on drawy board where Flash range (wide pre: broadways, Ax, suited connectors, clubs/spades) hits often. Check to realize fold equity post-turn or control.

On the turn, the board is 4c Jc Ks 3s. I have 8-high and no draw. Pre-flop I was the aggressor, but I checked back the flop. Elon has checked to me twice. While I could potentially represent a King or a slow-played hand, this board is quite static and favors the range of the Big Blind caller if they have a piece. I have zero equity with my current hand. However, Elon's double check often signifies weakness. That said, betting now without a draw is high variance. Since I have position and checked the flop, I will check behind again to either see a cheap showdown if I hit a pair on the river or to potentially bluff if a scary card comes.

River OOP with T7o (nothing: ten-high on paired 4s board KsJc4c3s4d, no pair/draw/SDV). Flash checked flop/turn behind checks, suggesting weakness (missed clubs/spades draws, weak K/J, mid-pairs/A-high) rather than traps/monsters. Pot 650 deep (~120BB eff.). Betting pure air lacks fold equity vs wide BTN range that showdowns often; gets called by all better (most hands). No worse hands to value. Check to realize equity via showdown (beats pure bluffs/A-high worse than T), pot control OOP, avoid spew late (110/150). Prior thoughts consistent: control, preserve stack. Optimal EV play.

On a board of 4c Jc Ks 3s 4d, I have 8-high, which holds zero showdown value against Elon's range. Elon has checked three times, suggesting he lacks a strong hand like a King or a Jack. By betting 400 into a 650 pot, I am representing a hand with some value that checked back for pot control or a 4 that improved on the river. Given Elon's recent tendency to fold to pressure when he hasn't shown strength, a bluff here is likely to succeed and secure the pot.

River OOP facing 400 bet into 1050 pot (~38% pot) with Td7s (ten-high, no pair, no draw, no SDV on paired 4s board KsJc4c3s4d). Flash checked flop/turn behind my checks, polarizing river bet to thin value (Kx, Jx, 44, KQ, etc.) or bluffs (missed clubs/spades draws like AcXc, QsXs). Calling requires ~28% equity (400/(1050+400)), but ten-high crushes only pure air (rare from checking range) and loses to all pairs/A-high/Q-high showdowns common from wide BTN preflop range. Raising air lacks fold equity OOP deep (~120BB eff., hand 110/150). Prior plan was check/call showdown if weak, but bet size/value-heavy range tilts EV negative. Opponent history shows aggression but not excessive river bluffing post-checks. Preserve stack, avoid spew with trash.

Button with T5s suited on a deep stack — this is a clear spot to open for position and steal the blinds. I’ll raise to a standard 2.5–3x BB to take the initiative and play the pot in position. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold this marginal hand; if I get one caller I’ll proceed postflop with C-bets on favorable textures and pot control otherwise. A 300 raise is consistent with table sizing and gives good fold equity while keeping stacks deep for postflop play.

Pocket 4s is a premium hand 3-handed against a button open, which is very wide. Facing a minraise to 300 (3BB), I'm getting good odds to continue but prefer to 3-bet isolate the loose button raiser rather than set mine OOP potentially multiway with BB behind. BB likely folds a wide range to a 3-bet. 3-bet to 9BB (900) is a standard aggressive sizing deepstacked to fold out worse or build pot with equity advantage when called. If shoved on, easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Six offsuit. Minni opened from the button and Elon 3-bet from the Small Blind. This situation is difficult for A6o. While the hand has some high card value, it is likely dominated by Elon's 3-betting range, which tends to be quite strong in 3-way pots from the small blind. Calling would put me out of position against two aggressive opponents in a bloated pot. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities as the tournament concludes.

Elon 3-bet to 900 after my BTN open to 300. T5s is marginal but has good postflop playability and I have position — with deep stacks I prefer to see a flop rather than fold or thin 4-bet bluff. I'll call the additional 600 (total 900 committed) to realize equity and use position to control the pot: c-bet selectively on dry boards, barrel/double-barrel as a semi-bluff with spade/straight equity, and fold to big, polarized aggression when I miss. I will only commit more chips on the turn/river with strong made hands or the best draws/implied odds situations.

Preflop 3-bet with 44 was standard to isolate vs button open. Flop 8c-3d-8h is a paired board that heavily favors Minni's 3-bet calling range from the button, which includes 88+, any 8x, overpairs JJ+, and some broadway/Ax that have showdown value or draws. My pocket 4s is an underpair with minimal showdown equity OOP heads-up deepstacked; it's behind most of her value range and vulnerable to any bet. No strong draws complete (possible weak 54 gutshot), board dry. C-betting thin risks bloating pot OOP with bad equity; better to check for pot control, induce bluffs from missed Ax/broadway, or check-fold to aggression realizing I'm crushed too often. With pot 19BB and deep stacks, preserve stack by playing post-flop cautiously rather than forcing action.

I called Elon's 3-bet preflop to play in position, but this flop (8c 3d 8h) completely misses my T5s — no pair and only a backdoor spade possibility. Elon checked; that can be weakness or a slow-played strong hand. With so little equity and no realistic immediate draw, a c-bet here would be a thin bluff that will get called by much of his 3-bet range and isn’t high EV. I’ll control the pot and realize equity in position: check and see the turn. If a spade or a Ten (or another favorable card) hits I can pursue aggression; if Elon fires a meaningful bet I’ll fold the majority of the time.

Turn board Js adds a broadway card, potentially completing gutshots like 10-9 or improving Jx in Minni's strong 3-bet calling range (JJ+, 88+, AQs+, AKo, sets, etc.). My 44 remains an underpair with no improved equity, drawing dead to runner-runner trips or better. OOP heads-up deepstacked (pot 19BB, stacks ~120BB+), betting risks bloating the pot with minimal showdown value or fold equity against her sticky range; thin value bets unlikely as 44 rarely best. Checking maintains pot control, induces bluffs from her missed overpairs/Ax/broadway, allows cheap river if checked behind, and easy fold to aggression realizing equity disadvantage on paired board. Preflop aggression extracted value pre; postflop caution preserves stack long-term in tournament.

Board on the turn is 8♣ 3♦ 8♥ J♠ and I hold T♠5♠. I have no pair and, importantly, no realistic draw: because the flop had zero spades, the J♠ on the turn does not allow me to make a five‑card spade flush by the river. My only meaningful outs are the three remaining Tens and three remaining Fives (~6 outs ≈ 13% to hit on the river), which is thin. Elon 3-bet preflop so his range is skewed strong; his check‑check line on flop/turn can be either weakness or a slowplay, but against a 3‑bettor a single-card bluff on the turn is low EV and likely to be called by many parts of his calling/raising range (Jx, pocket pairs, slow‑played 8x). I’ll control the pot and see the river for free — check and re-evaluate on the river (if I pair a Ten or Five I’ll consider a small value bet; otherwise I’ll fold to significant aggression).

River 9c completes some two-pair combinations (J9, 98, 39 unlikely) and trips with 99, but no flush or straight possible. My pocket 4s is a weak underpair OOP heads-up deepstacked (pot 19BB, stacks 120BB+). Minni's button call of my SB 3-bet OOP suggests a strong range: QQ+, AK/AQ/AJs+, KQs, medium pairs 55+, 88 (quads on flop). Prior checks through flop/turn indicate pot control with showdown value (overpairs/Ax high) rather than monsters, but she still beats me frequently with Jx, 9x, 8x, improved pairs. Betting thin for value lacks fold equity against her sticky range and risks bloating pot OOP with poor equity; she'll call better or fold worse. Checking allows cheap showdown, induces bluffs from Ax/broadway misses (possible after checks), and preserves stack by folding to aggression if she values bets improved hands. Preflop value extracted; postflop caution maximizes EV long-term.

I missed the river — I have T5s with a board of 8♣ 3♦ 8♥ J♠ 9♣ and no pair, no realistic draws. Elon 3-bet preflop so his range is skewed to strong hands (overpairs, JJ/TT, AK/AQ) and his check‑check line on flop/turn/river is consistent with either a slow‑played value or comfortable showdown hands; a small bluff here is unlikely to get many folds and is low EV. Pot is only 1,900 and there’s nothing to be gained by barreling a thin bluff that will be called by most of his range. I’ll avoid giving him a free decision to check‑raise and conserve chips — check and fold to significant river aggression.

3-handed on the button with Ts3d after SB folds and BB checks. While button range is wide and there's 150 in the pot (including dead money from SB), Ts3d is too weak: large gap, offsuit, poor post-flop playability, dominated by better broadway or pairing hands. BB (Minni) defends reasonably wide based on history, so risk-reward not favorable for a steal attempt. Preserve stack for better spots with 39 hands remaining in deep-stacked tournament.

I have KJs in the small blind. Elon has folded on the button. This is a clear raising hand in a small blind vs big blind situation to attack the pot and put pressure on Minni. I will make a standard raise to 300 total.

I’m in the BB with 8h7h suited facing a 3x SB open to 300. It costs me 200 to call (pot 400), so calling gives pot odds of ~33% and 8‑7s has good implied odds and post‑flop playability against a typical SB opening range. I don’t want to 3‑bet a marginal suited connector out of position — a raise here forfeits positional disadvantage and bloats the pot without a strong hand. With deep stacks I can realize equity and play post‑flop (continue with straights/flushes, two pair+, or good float spots; fold to heavy aggression on dry boards). Therefore I’ll call and see the flop.

On this flop of Jc-5s-2s, I have top pair with a King kicker. This is a very strong hand in a blind-versus-blind confrontation. I will lead out with a continuation bet to get value from weaker Jacks, middle pairs, and to charge any spade flush draws or straight draws. A bet of 350 into the 600 pot is a standard size that accomplishes these goals.

Flop: 5♠ J♣ 2♠ — I have 8♥7♥. Flash opened preflop from SB and made a small C-bet of 350 into a 950 pot. Calling 350 gives me roughly 26–27% pot odds. I have no pair and only backdoor heart/runner-runner straight possibilities, so raw equity is marginal, but Flash’s small C‑bet and wide SB opening range mean decent implied odds if I hit. Raising OOP with no pair and only backdoors is a bad play — it bloats the pot and hands me easy decisions vs a 3‑bet or a call. I’ll take the cheap look: call 350 to see the turn, but plan to fold to heavy turn aggression unless I pair, pick up a heart (flush draw) or otherwise improve to a strong draw/value hand.

The Queen on the turn is an overcard to my pair of Jacks, which slightly degrades the strength of my hand. Since I'm out of position and want to avoid playing a bloated pot against a potentially better hand like Qx or AJ, I will check. This allows me to control the pot size, see if Minni checks behind with a weaker hand, or decide whether to call a bet if she tries to represent the Queen or continues with a draw.

Flash checked the turn after a small flop c‑bet, and I have nothing — 8♥7♥ did not pair. Important: the flop had no hearts, so even with Q♥ on turn a river heart cannot give me a five‑card heart flush (I need three hearts on board). I only have ~6 outs to a pair (~13% to improve on the river), so betting OOP here would be a thin bluff that bloats the pot and plays poorly if called. With deep stacks and a weak holding I’ll control the pot and see the river for free — check and plan to fold to sizable river aggression unless I pair or get a very favorable action/read.

On the river, the board has paired the Jack, giving me three of a kind (Jacks) with a King kicker. The turn was checked through, suggesting Minni might not have a Queen. With no straights or flushes possible, my trips are likely the best hand. I will bet for value, hoping to get called by a Queen, a smaller pair, or even an Ace-high that doesn't believe I have the Jack after checking the turn.

Fold. I have 8♥7♥ — no pair, no completed draw. Line: Flash opened pre, small flop c‑bet (I called), both checked the Q♥ on the turn, then Flash bets 850 into a 2150 pot on the river after the J♦ pairs the board. Calling 850 requires ~28% equity, but my actual showdown equity with just eight‑high is essentially zero (I only beat rare pure bluffs). Flash’s line and sizing are consistent with value hands (Jx/trips, Qx, two‑pair/overpairs) and I don’t expect enough bluffs in his range to justify a call. Raising would be a pure OOP bluff with poor fold equity (min raise to 1700) and is unnecessarily risky. With a healthy stack I preserve chips and fold.

I am holding Seven of Diamonds and Trey of Spades (7-3 offsuit) on the button. This is one of the weakest hands in poker and does not warrant an opening raise, even in a short-handed game. My goal is to maximize my stack over 150 hands, and playing such a low-equity hand would be unnecessarily risky. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

2♣4♥ is one of the weakest holdings. I’m in the small blind out of position versus only the big blind (Elon), who defends and plays aggressively a lot — so this hand will be dominated or put in very tough post‑flop spots. Calling 50 to see a flop is a cheap but losing line long‑term and raising to steal is too thin with deep stacks. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

Fold. On the button with 7♦5♠ in a 3‑handed pot this is a weak, marginal hand. To call 100 into a 150 pot you need roughly 40% equity to break even — 7‑5 offsuit has much lower equity multiway and plays poorly out of position post‑flop. A min‑raise to 200 as a steal is possible, but both opponents have shown willingness to defend and Elon is frequently aggressive, so raising risks a 3‑bet or a difficult multiway pot. With a deep stack (~140 BB) there’s no need to gamble — preserve chips for a better spot.

AQs is a premium hand, ace-high suited with good playability. Button has folded, leaving us heads-up against the Big Blind. From the Small Blind, we have position post-flop and our hand crushes the BB's likely calling range (which includes many weaker aces, suited connectors, pairs). Raising builds the pot with a strong hand and applies pressure to potentially take down the blinds immediately. Standard sizing is 3x the BB to 300 total, which is aggressive but appropriate given the strength and dynamics. Deep stacks allow for post-flop flexibility if called.

In a Blind vs. Blind situation, 9-7 suited is a strong enough hand to defend against a standard 3x open from the Small Blind. I am getting 2:1 odds on the call and the hand has good post-flop playability and equity realization. I will call to see the flop.

Preflop raise with AQo was standard from SB to steal blinds or build pot OOP. Flop Td6cTs is a paired board that heavily favors villain's BB calling range, which includes Tx hands (AT, KT, QT, suited connectors like J9s, 87s drawing to straights), small-medium pairs, and weaker Ax.
